flashcards.q: מה ארבעת הקריטריונים של CAM לאבחון דליריום?
flashcards.a: 1) התחלה חריפה ותנודתי; 2) חוסר קשב; 3) חשיבה לא מאורגנת; 4) שינוי במצב הכרה. לאבחנה: (1)+(2) וגם (3) או (4).
איתור גורם הפיך; התערבויות לא-תרופתיות (אוריינטציה, שינה, משקפיים/שמיעה, ניידות); תרופות רק במצבי סיכון.

flashcards.back: 1) Acute onset/fluctuating course, 2) Inattention, 3) Disorganized thinking, 4) Altered level of consciousness. Need 1+2 plus either 3 or 4. Sensitivity 94-100%, specificity 90-95%.

flashcards.front: Yale Delirium Prevention Trial (HELP) — 6 risk factors addressed
flashcards.back: Cognitive impairment, sleep deprivation, immobility, vision impairment, hearing impairment, dehydration. Standardized protocols significantly reduce delirium incidence.

flashcards.front: 4 independent baseline risk factors for hospital delirium
flashcards.back: 1) Vision impairment, 2) Severe illness (APACHE II >16), 3) Cognitive impairment (MMSE <24), 4) BUN/Cr ratio ≥18. With ≥3 factors: 32-83% delirium risk.

flashcards.front: Hypoactive vs hyperactive delirium in elderly
flashcards.back: Hypoactive is MORE COMMON in elderly (slowed movement, paucity of speech, unresponsiveness) but identified far LESS frequently. Hyperactive: restlessness, agitation, constant movement.
Does NOT prevent postoperative delirium. RCTs in elective joint replacement and hip replacement showed no difference vs placebo (SOE=A). Rivastigmine also ineffective (SOE=B).
ED: 6-38% of adults >65. Hospitalized: 10-50%. ICU: approaches 75%. Unrecognized in up to 70% of hospitalized elderly (SOE=B).
4AT, bCAM, and RASS more favorably balance brevity, accuracy, and ED provider acceptability than full CAM, which requires careful training and specific protocol.

flashcards.front: Delirium as 'stress test for the brain'
flashcards.back: If a previously healthy person develops delirium from a minor insult (UTI, elective surgery), suspect unrecognized underlying neurologic disease. Screen for dementia after delirium clears.

mcqs.q: איזה מרכיב אינו חלק מ-CAM?
mcqs.explanation: CAM כולל הופעה חריפה/תנודתיות, חוסר קשב, חשיבה לא מאורגנת, שינוי במצב הכרה.

mcqs.q: מה ההבדל המרכזי בין דליריום והתחלה של דמנציה?
options: דליריום – התחלה חריפה ותנודתי; דמנציה – הדרגתית
mcqs.correct: דליריום – התחלה חריפה ותנודתי; דמנציה – הדרגתית
mcqs.explanation: דליריום מתפתח במהירות ונוטה לתנודתיות; דמנציה מתקדמת לאט.

mcqs.q: איזה מהם הוא טריגר שכיח לדליריום?
mcqs.explanation: נטל אנטיכולינרגי גבוה מעלה סיכון לדליריום.

mcqs.q: איזו התערבות לא-תרופתית מומלצת כחלק מטיפול בדליריום?
mcqs.explanation: תמיכה חושית ואוריינטציה משפרות קשב והכוונה.

mcqs.q: באיזה מצב נשקול טיפול פרמקולוגי בדליריום?
mcqs.explanation: תרופות נשקלות רק אם יש סכנה או אי-שיתוף פעולה מסכן.

mcqs.q: מה נדרש ל-CAM חיובי?
mcqs.explanation: קריטריוני CAM הקלאסיים.

mcqs.q: איזה בדיקת סקר מהירה מתמקדת בחוסר קשב בדליריום?
mcqs.explanation: CAM מזהה חוסר קשב כחלק מליבה.

mcqs.q: איזה מאפיין פחות אופייני לדליריום היפואקטיבי?
mcqs.explanation: אי‑שקט אופייני יותר לתת‑סוג ההיפראקטיבי.

mcqs.q: איזה צעד מניעתי סביבתי מומלץ למטופל בסיכון לדליריום?
mcqs.explanation: אוריינטציה מתמדת מסייעת.

mcqs.q: איזה גורם מעבדה יכול לתרום לדליריום?
mcqs.explanation: הפרעות אלקטרוליטריות שכיחות.

mcqs.q: אצל מי הסיכון לדליריום גבוה במיוחד?
mcqs.explanation: דמנציה היא פקטור סיכון מרכזי.

mcqs.q: איזו תרופה פחות מתאימה לטיפול באי‑שקט בדליריום?
mcqs.explanation: בנזודיאזפינים עלולים להחמיר דליריום.

mcqs.q: מהו מאפיין זמן של דליריום לעומת דיכאון?
mcqs.explanation: Fluctuation סימן אופייני.

mcqs.q: איזו התערבות משפחתית מומלצת?
mcqs.explanation: תורם לאוריינטציה ורוגע.

mcqs.q: מהו הבדל אופייני בדליריום לעומת פסיכוזה ראשונית?
mcqs.explanation: שינוי הכרה אינו מאפיין פסיכוזה ראשונית.

mcqs.q: מהי בדיקה בסיסית בכל חולה עם דליריום?
mcqs.explanation: חיפוש סיבות נפוצות והפיכות.

mcqs.q: איזו התערבות שינה מומלצת?
mcqs.explanation: היגיינת שינה מפחיתה דליריום.

mcqs.q: איזה מצב זיהומי שכיח כטריגר?
mcqs.explanation: טריגרים שכיחים בקשישים.

mcqs.q: איזה כלי מיון עשוי לזהות סיכון מוקדם?
mcqs.explanation: כלי סקר מהיר לדליריום.

mcqs.q: איזה פרופיל דליריום פחות זוהה לעיתים?
mcqs.explanation: נראה שקט ולכן מוחמץ.

description: Delirium is an acute confusional state with fluctuating consciousness, inattention, and cognitive dysfunction. It affects 10-50% of hospitalized elderly and is often unrecognized (up to 70%). Key tools: CAM, 4AT, bCAM. Prevention via HELP protocol addressing 6 risk factors. Hypoactive form most common in elderly.

flashcards.q: איזו הדמיה רלוונטית בהערכת דמנציה בשגרה?
flashcards.a: CT/MRI מוח לשלול גורמים הפיכים/מבניים.
פלאקים עמילואידיים וסבכים נוירופיברילריים של טאו.

flashcards.front: Dementia with Lewy Bodies — distinguishing features
flashcards.back: Fluctuating cognition, prominent visual hallucinations, parkinsonism, attentional deficits resembling delirium. Cholinergic deficiency from basal forebrain degeneration. Extremely vulnerable to delirium.
Central auditory dysfunction is a PRECURSOR to incident dementia (SOE=A). Greater hearing loss independently associated with lower MMSE, memory, and executive function scores.
Reduction in cognitive performance from 25-dBHL hearing loss is equivalent to 6.8 years of aging on mental status, memory, and executive function measures.

flashcards.front: Depression screening in dementia — which tool?
flashcards.back: Cornell Depression Scale for Dementia: completed by CAREGIVERS, does not require patient insight. Score ≥6: 93% sensitivity, 97% specificity for depression in dementia.
Sertraline and mirtazapine NO better than placebo for depression in Alzheimer's at 13 and 39 weeks. Adverse effects >40% on antidepressants vs 25% placebo. Recommend 3-month watchful waiting first.

flashcards.front: Anticholinesterase inhibitors for AD behavioral symptoms
flashcards.back: Donepezil is the most prudent initial choice for episodic paranoid symptoms in recently diagnosed AD (SOE=C), before considering antipsychotics.

1) Understand risks/benefits/alternatives, 2) Reason about information in context of values, 3) Appreciate consequences of choice, 4) Make and communicate a choice. Patient need NOT agree with physician.

flashcards.front: Over one-third of all-cause dementia risk is associated with:
flashcards.back: Hearing loss. Study showed >1/3 of all-cause dementia risk was associated with hearing loss. Self-reported hearing aid use was NOT associated with significant dementia risk reduction.

mcqs.q: מטרת ההדמיה המבנית בדמנציה?
mcqs.explanation: האבחנה קלינית; ההדמיה שוללת גורמים אחרים.

mcqs.q: מה הקוגניציה הראשונית שנפגעת באלצהיימר טיפוסי?
mcqs.explanation: פגיעה בזיכרון לטווח קצר/אפיזודי מוקדמת.

mcqs.q: מה מטרת CT/MRI בהערכת דמנציה?
mcqs.explanation: תומך בתהליך האבחוני.

mcqs.q: איזה ממצא פתולוגי אופייני?
mcqs.explanation: שקיעת Aβ וטאו.

mcqs.q: איזה כלי סינון קוגניטיבי נפוץ?
mcqs.explanation: שימושי בקהילה ובאשפוז.

mcqs.q: איזה מצב עלול לחקות דמנציה?
mcqs.explanation: ירידה חדה ומהירה בתפקוד קוגניטיבי.

mcqs.q: איזו דמנציה מתבטאת בהפרעות חזותיות מוקדמות והלוצינציות?
mcqs.explanation: הלוצינציות חזותיות מוקדמות אופייניות.

mcqs.q: איזה מאפיין תומך בדמנציה וסקולרית?
options: ירידה מדרדרת בצעדים לאחר אירועים מוחיים
mcqs.correct: ירידה מדרדרת בצעדים לאחר אירועים מוחיים
mcqs.explanation: course סטפ‑לייק.

mcqs.q: מה ההבדל בין MCI לדמנציה?
mcqs.explanation: דמנציה כרוכה בתפקוד ירוד.

mcqs.q: איזה בדיקות דם בסיסיות חשוב לשלול?
mcqs.explanation: חסר B12 ותת‑תריסיות הפיכות.

mcqs.q: איזה טיפול תרופתי עשוי להועיל באלצהיימר קל‑בינוני?
mcqs.explanation: מעכבי AChE.

mcqs.q: מה נכון לגבי ממנטין?
mcqs.explanation: אנטגוניסט NMDA.

mcqs.q: איזה מצב עלול לדמות דמנציה?
mcqs.explanation: Acute confusional state.

mcqs.q: איזו גישה לא רצויה להתנהגויות BPSD?
mcqs.explanation: לא‑תרופתי תחילה.

mcqs.q: איזה דימוי מוחי עדיף כשאפשר?
mcqs.explanation: רגישות טובה לשינויים מבניים.

mcqs.q: מה אופייני ל‑FTD?
mcqs.explanation: פגיעה פרה‑פרונטלית/טמפורלית.

mcqs.q: איזה כלי הערכה קצר מומלץ בקהילה?
mcqs.explanation: רגיש ל‑MCI.

mcqs.q: מה נכון לגבי דמנציה עם גופיפי לואי ביחס לנוירולפטיים טיפוסיים?
mcqs.explanation: Neuroleptic sensitivity.

mcqs.q: איזו התערבות מפחיתה Wandering?
mcqs.explanation: שגרה ו‑safe wandering.

mcqs.q: מה ההבדל בין BPSD לדיכאון מאוחר?
mcqs.explanation: מאפיינים נפרדים.

description: Dementia is a chronic progressive decline in cognition affecting function. Alzheimer disease accounts for 60-80% of cases. Key assessments: MMSE, MoCA, clock drawing. Cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) for mild-moderate; memantine for moderate-severe. Behavioral symptoms managed non-pharmacologically first.

flashcards.a: ירידה במשקל, עייפות, חולשה, הליכה איטית, פעילות נמוכה. שלושה מהם = שבריריות.
סולם קליני (1–9) להערכת שבריריות – מנבא תמותה ותוצאות.

flashcards.back: ≥3 of: 1) Unintentional weight loss (>10 lbs/year), 2) Self-reported exhaustion, 3) Low physical activity, 4) Slow walking speed, 5) Weak grip strength. 1-2 = pre-frail.
Medical, cognitive, affective/mood, social, economic, environmental, spiritual, functional status, frailty, advance care planning. Instruments guide but don't replace clinical judgment.

flashcards.front: Geriatric assessment differs from standard evaluation by:
flashcards.back: Emphasizes functional status as both dimension to evaluate AND outcome to improve. Includes social, psychological, environmental factors beyond disease manifestations.
Web-based WHO calculator integrating clinical risk factors + optional femoral neck BMD. Gives 10-year probability of hip and major osteoporotic fracture. Treatment threshold: hip ≥3% or major ≥20%.

flashcards.front: Vitamin D and fall prevention — USPSTF
flashcards.back: Vitamin D supplementation + exercise are specific interventions reducing falls. USPSTF meta-analysis: vitamin D reduces falls by 17%. Osteoporosis Canada meta-analysis confirmed.

flashcards.front: Physical fitness vs weight loss in older adults
flashcards.back: Physical fitness and functional independence confer MORE benefit than weight loss alone. No mortality benefit from weight loss proven in older adults. Combined exercise + weight loss best for obese elderly.
Identify and avoid low-value interventions in geriatric care. Single component of larger value-based care constellation. Doesn't replace clinical judgment but guides appropriate resource use.

flashcards.front: Proactive geriatric consultation to prevent delirium
flashcards.back: Protocol-based: pain treatment, eliminate unnecessary meds, bowel/bladder regulation, early mobilization. Effective at reducing delirium incidence (SOE=B).

mcqs.q: איזו הצהרה נכונה לגבי Fried?
mcqs.explanation: Fried מבוסס תפקודי בלבד.

mcqs.q: כמה קריטריונים נדרשים כדי להגדיר שבריריות לפי Fried?
mcqs.explanation: ≥3 קריטריונים = שבריר, 1–2 = טרום-שבריר.

mcqs.q: איזו התערבות הראתה שיפור תפקודי בשבריריות?
mcqs.explanation: שילוב פעילות+חלבון יעיל.

mcqs.q: מהו CFS?
mcqs.explanation: Clinical Frailty Scale של Rockwood.

mcqs.q: איזה קריטריון אינו חלק מפנוטיפ Fried?
mcqs.explanation: פנוטיפ Fried הוא תפקודי-פיזי.

mcqs.q: מה המשמעות הקלינית של שבריריות?
mcqs.explanation: שבריריות מנבאת תוצאות גרועות.

mcqs.q: מהו ערך אחיזת יד חלש אופייני בשבריריות?
mcqs.explanation: מדדים תלויי מגדר/גובה.

mcqs.q: איזו בדיקה פונקציונלית מסייעת בהערכת איטיות?
mcqs.explanation: Timed Up and Go.

mcqs.q: מהי תועלת Nutritional supplementation בשבריריות?
mcqs.explanation: סינרגיה עם אימון.

mcqs.q: מה ה‑Clinical Frailty Scale 7 מרמז?
mcqs.explanation: CFS 7≈חומרה גבוהה.

mcqs.q: איזו תופעה אינה קשורה לשבריריות?
mcqs.explanation: אין קשר ישיר.

mcqs.q: מהו קריטריון הירידה במשקל בפריד?
mcqs.explanation: זה הקריטריון הקלאסי.

mcqs.q: איזו התערבות סביבתית מסייעת לבריאות תפקודית?
mcqs.explanation: משפר ניידות ובטיחות.

mcqs.q: איזו בדיקת סקר תזונתי רלוונטית?
mcqs.explanation: Mini Nutritional Assessment.

mcqs.q: מהי תדירות מומלצת לאימוני התנגדות לקשישים?
mcqs.explanation: המלצה נפוצה.

mcqs.q: מה קשר בין שבריריות ונפילות?
mcqs.explanation: קשר דו‑כיווני.

mcqs.q: איזה מדד שימושי ליציבות בהליכה?
mcqs.explanation: קשור לסיכון לנפילות.

mcqs.q: איזה מרכיב אינו מומלץ כניסוי לשיפור שבריריות?
mcqs.explanation: פוגעת בתפקוד.

mcqs.q: מה המשמעות של Pre‑frail (1–2 קריטריונים)?
mcqs.explanation: ניתן להפחית התדרדרות.

mcqs.q: איזו אסטרטגיה רב‑תחומית יעילה?
mcqs.explanation: גישה CGA.

description: Frailty is a clinical syndrome of decreased physiologic reserve and increased vulnerability. Fried phenotype: ≥3 of weight loss, exhaustion, low activity, slow gait, weak grip. Associated with falls, disability, hospitalization, and death. Interventions: exercise (especially resistance training), nutrition optimization, comprehensive geriatric assessment.

flashcards.q: מה ההתערבות היעילה ביותר למניעת נפילות בקהילה?
flashcards.a: תרגולי שיווי משקל וצעידה מותאמת.

flashcards.q: איזה סוג תרופות מחייב הפחתה עקב סיכון נפילות?
flashcards.a: בנזודיאזפינים ותרופות פסיכואקטיביות.

flashcards.back: Health IT-based nursing-led falls prevention toolkit: bed posters, patient education handouts, plans of care. RCT showed effectiveness for patient-specific education and communication.
1) Fall history (3 months), 2) Secondary diagnosis, 3) Ambulatory aid, 4) IV/heparin lock, 5) Gait, 6) Mental status. Scored to determine low/moderate/high fall risk.

flashcards.front: Diabetes and fall risk in elderly
flashcards.back: DM increases fall risk via peripheral neuropathy, hypoglycemia, orthostatic hypotension, visual impairment. Clinicians must provide fall risk education and guided physical therapy mobility programs.
Essential in elderly: detects orthostatic hypotension (common with antihypertensives, diuretics, autonomic dysfunction). More important in geriatric exam than many traditional elements.

flashcards.front: In-hospital delirium risk factors that increase falls
flashcards.back: Physical restraints, bladder catheterization, sleep/sensory deprivation, ≥3 new medications. Avoiding these prevents both delirium AND falls.

flashcards.front: Physical exam in hospitalized elderly — priorities
flashcards.back: Day 5 of hospitalization: more important to see if patient can SIT UP/STAND than to listen to heart sounds. Postural BP, cognitive status, hearing, vision, foot exam are high-yield.
Calluses over bunions (hallux valgus) common in elderly. In diabetic patients: DEBRIDE and assess for ulcer first before padding or shoe changes. Untreated foot problems increase fall risk.
Two RCTs in hip surgery patients ≥70: no significant reduction in delirium incidence (SOE=A), but severity and duration were reduced. Not recommended for routine prevention.

mcqs.q: מה עמדת ה-USPSTF לגבי ויטמין D למניעת נפילות?
mcqs.explanation: אין ראיות ליעילות במניעת נפילות ברוב המבוגרים בקהילה.

mcqs.q: איזו התערבות הראתה את הראיות החזקות ביותר למניעת נפילות בקהילה?
mcqs.explanation: בהתאם ל‑USPSTF.

mcqs.q: איזו תרופה מעלה סיכון לנפילות?
mcqs.explanation: השפעה סדטיבית.

mcqs.q: מהו צעד בסיסי בהערכת נפילה?
mcqs.explanation: הערכת גורמים ניתנים לשינוי.

mcqs.q: מה עמדת USPSTF לגבי ויטמין D למניעת נפילות?
mcqs.explanation: אין תועלת במניעת נפילות ברוב הבוגרים בקהילה.

mcqs.q: איזה שינוי סביבתי עשוי להפחית נפילות?
mcqs.explanation: התאמות סביבתיות מסייעות.

mcqs.q: איזה גורם סיכון פנימי לנפילות?
mcqs.explanation: פקטור ביולוגי.

mcqs.q: איזה בדיקת סקר נפוצה לניידות?
mcqs.explanation: Timed Up and Go.

mcqs.q: איזה שינוי תרופתי יכול להפחית סיכון?
mcqs.explanation: מפחית סחרחורת ונמנום.

mcqs.q: איזה עזר ניידות מסייע?
mcqs.explanation: משפר יציבות.

mcqs.q: באיזו סביבה יש יותר נפילות?
mcqs.explanation: מרבית הנפילות מתרחשות בבית.

mcqs.q: איזו בדיקת ראייה קריטית?
mcqs.explanation: ליקוי ראייה קשור לנפילות.

mcqs.q: איזה מצב אורטוסטטי מגביר נפילות?
mcqs.explanation: גורם לסחרחורת בעמידה.

mcqs.q: איזו התערבות קהילתית יעילה?
mcqs.explanation: משפר איזון וגמישות.

mcqs.q: מהי המלצת תדירות לאימוני שיווי משקל?
mcqs.explanation: נתמך בהמלצות מניעה.

mcqs.q: איזה ציוד בטיחותי מומלץ בחדר אמבטיה?
mcqs.explanation: מצמצם החלקה.

mcqs.q: מהי תועלת הערכה תרופתית ביתית?
mcqs.explanation: סקירה סביבתית/תרופתית מועילה.

mcqs.q: איזה מצב לבבי עשוי לגרום לנפילה פתאומית?
mcqs.explanation: סינקופה.

mcqs.q: איזה מינרל/ויטמין **אינו** מומלץ שגרתי למניעת נפילות?
mcqs.explanation: לא מומלץ שגרתי למניעה בקהילה.

mcqs.q: איזה אימון משפר כוח גס ותמיכה?
mcqs.explanation: מחזק שרירים.

description: Falls affect ~30% of community-dwelling elderly annually. Multifactorial: muscle weakness, gait/balance disorders, polypharmacy, environmental hazards, visual impairment. Assessment: Timed Up and Go, Morse Fall Scale. Prevention: multifactorial interventions, exercise, medication review, vitamin D, environmental modification.

flashcards.q: מה הטיפול הקו הראשון בדיכאון קשישים ומה הסיכון לניטור?
flashcards.a: SSRI (סרטרלין/ציטלופרם); לנטר היפונתרמיה (SIADH).

flashcards.q: איזו גישה פסיכותרפית יעילה לאחר גיל 65?
flashcards.a: CBT, כולל קבוצתי.

flashcards.back: 1) Little interest or pleasure in doing things? 2) Feeling down, depressed, or hopeless? Over past 2 weeks. If positive → proceed to PHQ-9.
Score >10: 88% sensitivity, 88% specificity for major depression. Reliable for detection and monitoring severity.
PHQ-2, PHQ-9, GDS (Geriatric Depression Scale), GDS-short form. Screen annually or when symptoms arise.

1-year stepped collaborative care: depression care manager in primary care, treatment alternatives (meds + problem-solving therapy), stepped-care algorithm, weekly psychiatrist/geriatrician supervision. Lower costs at 4 years.
326 patients, 9 centers. Sertraline & mirtazapine = placebo at 13 and 39 weeks. AEs >40% on drug vs 25% placebo. Recommend 3 months watchful waiting + psychosocial interventions first.
Caregiver-completed. Score ≥6: 93% sensitivity, 97% specificity. Use when patient lacks insight into mood (moderate-severe dementia).
~50% improvement rate (higher with sequential strategies) in elderly WITHOUT dementia. No comparable evidence exists for depressed patients WITH dementia.
6-8 session structured psychotherapy. Similarly effective to antidepressants for major depression in older primary care patients. One of the treatment alternatives offered.

mcqs.q: מה הסיכון התרופתי העיקרי של SSRI בקשישים?
mcqs.explanation: SSRIs גורמים ל־SIADH, במיוחד בתחילת הטיפול.
The PHQ-2 asks about depressed mood and anhedonia over 2 weeks. If positive, follow up with the full PHQ-9. The Cornell Scale is for depression in dementia specifically.
mcqs.q: Which screening tool is recommended as an initial brief screen for depression in older adults?

mcqs.explanation: PHQ-9 score >10 has 88% sensitivity and 88% specificity for major depression, making it a reliable screening and monitoring tool.
mcqs.q: A PHQ-9 score >10 has what sensitivity and specificity for major depression in older adults?

options: 3 months of watchful waiting with psychosocial interventions
mcqs.explanation: HTA-SADD showed no benefit of sertraline or mirtazapine vs placebo at 13 and 39 weeks in depression with dementia. Adverse effects occurred in >40% on antidepressants. Recommend 3 months observation with stepped psychosocial interventions first.
mcqs.q: An 84-year-old with Alzheimer's develops depressive symptoms. The HTA-SADD trial showed that first-line treatment should be:
mcqs.correct: 3 months of watchful waiting with psychosocial interventions

mcqs.explanation: The Cornell Scale is completed by caregivers and has 93% sensitivity and 97% specificity for depression in dementia (score ≥6). It doesn't require patient insight.
mcqs.q: Which depression screening tool is most appropriate for a patient with moderate dementia who lacks insight into their mood?

options: Additional time to adjust rather than treatment
Medications are needed for his vegetative symptoms
mcqs.explanation: Psychotherapy is appropriate first-line when preferred by the patient. Multiple forms of psychotherapy (CBT, problem-solving therapy, interpersonal therapy) are effective for late-life depression, not only CBT.
mcqs.q: A 79-year-old retired psychiatrist has insomnia, weight loss, anhedonia, and irritability for 2 months since retirement. He declines medication but would accept psychotherapy. What is the best recommendation?

options: Depression care manager in primary care
Weekly supervision by geriatrician and psychiatrist
mcqs.explanation: IMPACT offers patients treatment alternatives including antidepressants AND problem-solving therapy (6-8 sessions). It doesn't mandate medication. At 4-year follow-up, total healthcare costs were significantly lower.
mcqs.q: The IMPACT collaborative care model for late-life depression includes all EXCEPT:

options: Similarly effective to antidepressants for major depression
Effective only when combined with medication
mcqs.explanation: Problem-solving therapy is a 6-8 session structured psychotherapy found similarly effective to antidepressants in treating major depressive disorder in older primary care patients.
mcqs.q: Problem-solving therapy in the IMPACT model has been found to be:
mcqs.correct: Similarly effective to antidepressants for major depression

options: Reassure that minor forgetfulness is normal
mcqs.explanation: Forgetfulness, tiredness, and insomnia after a grief event could be symptoms of depression. Screen for depression first with GDS. If negative, proceed with cognitive screening.
mcqs.q: A 68-year-old presents with forgetfulness after his father's death from Alzheimer's. He also has insomnia and fatigue. The most appropriate next step is:

mcqs.explanation: Switching or augmenting yields about 50% improvement (even higher with persistent sequential strategies). However, there are no comparable findings for depressed patients WITH dementia.
mcqs.q: In depressed older patients WITHOUT dementia, switching or augmenting antidepressants yields approximately what improvement rate?

mcqs.explanation: Depression INCREASES mortality, disability, healthcare use, and decreases quality of life.
mcqs.q: Late-life depression is associated with all EXCEPT:

options: Sadness/feeling down and loss of interest
mcqs.explanation: Quick screen: 'Have you been sad/blue/down?' and 'Have you lost interest in things?' If positive, do full GDS or PHQ-9.
mcqs.q: The two-question depressive screen asks about:
mcqs.correct: Sadness/feeling down and loss of interest

options: Symptom overlap with PD motor features
Screening tools are invalid in PD
mcqs.explanation: Psychomotor slowing, flat affect, sleep disturbance in PD mimic depression. Comorbid cognitive impairment further complicates detection.
mcqs.q: Depression in Parkinson's disease is often underdiagnosed because:
mcqs.correct: Symptom overlap with PD motor features

mcqs.explanation: Grief usually does NOT cause memory problems or functional loss. When present, screen for depression, which may coexist with grief.
mcqs.q: Grief typically differs from major depression in that grief is NOT associated with:

mcqs.explanation: 9-25% develop persistent grief (DSM-5: 6-12 months duration). Associated with disability, cognitive impairment, suicidal thinking.
mcqs.q: Persistent/complicated grief affects what percentage of bereaved older adults?

options: Increased risk of cognitive impairment over 10 years
mcqs.explanation: Global Self-Reported Health correlates with objective indices (SOE=A). Poor ratings predict cognitive impairment risk even in community-dwelling adults 60-64.
mcqs.q: Self-reported poor health in adults ≥65 predicts:
mcqs.correct: Increased risk of cognitive impairment over 10 years

options: Bolstering sense of meaning and reducing existential distress
mcqs.explanation: Short-term psychotherapy supporting meaning-making in life-limiting illness. Patients with higher sense of meaning have less end-of-life distress.
mcqs.q: Dignity therapy at end of life improves well-being by:
mcqs.correct: Bolstering sense of meaning and reducing existential distress

mcqs.explanation: Elderly patients often present with somatic complaints (pain, GI symptoms, fatigue) and cognitive complaints ('pseudodementia') rather than classic sadness.
mcqs.q: Atypical depression in elderly may present primarily as:

options: Hyponatremia is a recognized adverse effect
They work faster than in younger patients
mcqs.explanation: SSRIs can cause SIADH-related hyponatremia, especially in elderly. Also risk of GI bleeding, falls, and insomnia. Monitor sodium in first weeks.
mcqs.q: Which is TRUE about SSRIs in elderly patients?
mcqs.correct: Hyponatremia is a recognized adverse effect

mcqs.explanation: Annual depression screening or when symptoms arise is recommended. Use PHQ-2, PHQ-9, GDS, or GDS-short form (evidence-supported tools).
mcqs.q: Depression screening in older adults is recommended:

description: Depression prevalence 10-30% in older adults. Often underdiagnosed due to atypical presentation and comorbidity. Screening: PHQ-2 → PHQ-9, GDS, Cornell Scale (for dementia). Treatment: psychotherapy (CBT, problem-solving therapy) and/or antidepressants. IMPACT collaborative care model shows sustained benefit.

flashcards.a: סקירה מלאה, הערכת תועלת/סיכון, סדר עדיפויות להפסקה, ניטור.
חוסר קווים ברורים, מחסור בתמיכה, התנגדות מטופלית.

flashcards.back: New medication prescribed to treat adverse effects of another medication. Example: oxybutynin started for urinary urgency caused by donepezil. Correct approach: discontinue the offending agent.

flashcards.front: Beers Criteria — key drug classes to avoid in elderly
flashcards.back: Anticholinergics, benzodiazepines, NSAIDs (especially with HF/CKD), first-generation antihistamines, barbiturates, meperidine, sliding-scale insulin (prolonged use).
Pharmacodynamic antagonism: each reduces the other's efficacy. Anticholinergics worsen cognition; cholinesterase inhibitors worsen bladder symptoms. Avoid concurrent use when possible.

flashcards.front: OTC decongestants in men with BPH
flashcards.back: Alpha-adrenergic agonist decongestants constrict bladder outlet smooth muscle → can precipitate acute urinary retention in men with BPH (SOE=A). Ask about OTC meds.
Cognitive adverse effects correlate with PEAK drug levels (SOE=A). Extended-release has lower peaks → potentially fewer cognitive effects than immediate-release.

flashcards.front: EQUiPPED protocol for ED medication safety
flashcards.back: Education + personal provider feedback + electronic clinical decision support. Sustained reduction in potentially inappropriate prescribing at 1 year across 4 VA hospitals.
Framing as gains → risk averse (save 200 people); as losses → risk seeking (400 die). Medical professionals equally susceptible. Affects advance care planning, treatment choices.
3.5 chronic diseases. One disease may obscure another's presentation. Treating one may worsen another. Disease-specific approach is not optimal in elderly.

mcqs.q: מה הגישה הנכונה לפוליפרמקיה בקשיש?
mcqs.explanation: גישה הדרגתית בהתאם לצרכים.
Acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil) have procholinergic effects that can cause urinary urgency/incontinence. Starting oxybutynin to treat this would be a prescribing cascade.
mcqs.q: An 84-year-old on donepezil develops new urinary urgency and incontinence. Which medication change is most likely responsible?

mcqs.explanation: A prescribing cascade occurs when a new medication is prescribed to treat adverse effects of another medication. The correct approach is to discontinue or reduce the offending agent.
mcqs.q: Starting oxybutynin to treat incontinence caused by donepezil is an example of:

mcqs.explanation: Oxybutynin cognitive adverse effects correlate with peak drug levels (SOE=A). Extended-release formulations with lower peak levels may have fewer cognitive effects than immediate-release.
mcqs.q: The cognitive adverse effects of oxybutynin are related to:

mcqs.explanation: OTC nasal decongestants are alpha-adrenergic agonists that constrict bladder outlet smooth muscle. In men with BPH, this increases dynamic resistance and can precipitate retention.
mcqs.q: A 76-year-old with BPH develops acute urinary retention after starting OTC cold medication. The most likely culprit is:

mcqs.explanation: Diphenhydramine has strong anticholinergic effects causing sedation, confusion, urinary retention, and constipation. Second-generation antihistamines (cetirizine, loratadine, fexofenadine) are preferred.
mcqs.q: Which anticholinergic medication used for allergies should be avoided in older adults according to Beers Criteria?

options: Pharmacodynamic antagonism reducing efficacy of both
Improved urinary function with stable cognition
mcqs.explanation: Anticholinergics and cholinesterase inhibitors have opposing mechanisms — they antagonize each other. Anticholinergics may worsen cognition, and cholinesterase inhibitors may worsen bladder symptoms.
mcqs.q: Concurrent use of anticholinergic bladder medications and cholinesterase inhibitors results in:
mcqs.correct: Pharmacodynamic antagonism reducing efficacy of both

options: May improve incontinence when alpha-blocker is maximized first
mcqs.explanation: Adding anticholinergic medications to alpha-blocker therapy may improve incontinence in men with BPH and overactive bladder (SOE=A), but alpha-blocker should be maximized first.
mcqs.q: Adding alpha-blocker therapy with anticholinergic bladder medication in men with BPH and overactive bladder:
mcqs.correct: May improve incontinence when alpha-blocker is maximized first

mcqs.explanation: For patients with advanced dementia, feeding tube placement has no clear benefit. Clinicians recommend it no longer appear as an option in advance care planning documents like POLST.
mcqs.q: POLST (Physician Orders for Life-Sustaining Therapies) forms for patients with advanced dementia increasingly exclude which option?

options: Stop all potentially inappropriate medications simultaneously
Taper one medication at a time and monitor
Only deprescribe medications on Beers list
Deprescribe only if the patient requests it
mcqs.explanation: Deprescribing should be done one medication at a time with monitoring for withdrawal effects or return of symptoms. This allows attribution of any changes to the specific medication stopped.
mcqs.q: When deprescribing in an elderly patient, which principle is MOST important?
mcqs.correct: Taper one medication at a time and monitor

mcqs.explanation: For episodic, recently diagnosed AD with behavioral symptoms, optimizing cholinesterase inhibitor is the most prudent first step (SOE=C) before adding antipsychotics.
mcqs.q: A 79-year-old with Alzheimer's on donepezil has episodic paranoid ideas. The most appropriate initial pharmacologic treatment is:

options: Mandatory pharmacy consult for all patients
Education, provider feedback, and electronic decision support
mcqs.explanation: EQUiPPED combines education + personal provider feedback + electronic clinical decision support. Demonstrated sustained reduction in potentially inappropriate prescribing at 1 year.
mcqs.q: In the EQUiPPED protocol for medication safety in EDs, the intervention combines:
mcqs.correct: Education, provider feedback, and electronic decision support

options: Apply exactly as in outpatient settings
May have different risk-benefit for short-term prescriptions
Are never relevant in acute care
Only apply to nursing home patients
mcqs.explanation: Beers Criteria generally apply to longer-duration prescriptions. Short ED prescriptions may have a unique risk-benefit profile that remains relatively untested.
mcqs.q: Beers Criteria in the ED setting:
mcqs.correct: May have different risk-benefit for short-term prescriptions

options: Initiate deprescribing and communicate to primary physicians
Only adjust if they are geriatricians
mcqs.explanation: ED clinicians should initiate deprescribing for overtreatment (hypoglycemia, hypotension) and communicate findings/recommendations to the patient's physicians.
mcqs.q: ED clinicians who identify overtreated hypertension or hyperglycemia should:
mcqs.correct: Initiate deprescribing and communicate to primary physicians

options: Age-related physiologic changes affect test interpretation and treatment responses
Evidence-based guidelines are identical for all ages
mcqs.explanation: Age-related changes affect diagnostics and treatment. Average 75-year-old has 3.5 chronic diseases; one disease may obscure another's presentation, and treating one may worsen another.
mcqs.q: Clinical decision making in older adults differs from younger patients primarily because:
mcqs.correct: Age-related physiologic changes affect test interpretation and treatment responses

mcqs.explanation: Good decision-making elicits patient priorities among competing domains (longevity vs comfort) to ensure preferences drive all evaluation and management decisions.
mcqs.q: In geriatric decision-making, patient priorities should focus on:

options: Affect both clinicians and patients equally
Can be eliminated by better education
mcqs.explanation: Prospect theory: when options framed as gains → risk averse; as losses → risk seeking. Medical professionals are equally susceptible to these biases.
mcqs.q: Framing effects in medical decision-making:
mcqs.correct: Affect both clinicians and patients equally

options: Reducing low-value care through evidence-based recommendations
mcqs.explanation: AGS Choosing Wisely guidelines identify low-value interventions to avoid, as one component of moving toward value-based care.
mcqs.q: The Choosing Wisely campaign in geriatrics addresses:
mcqs.correct: Reducing low-value care through evidence-based recommendations

options: The newest medication is always responsible
It becomes difficult to identify the causative change
All medications should be stopped immediately
mcqs.explanation: Making multiple medication changes at once makes it difficult to identify which change caused new symptoms. Change one at a time when possible.
mcqs.q: When multiple medication changes are made simultaneously and new symptoms develop:
mcqs.correct: It becomes difficult to identify the causative change

options: Anticholinergic effects are rare in elderly
Cumulative effects from multiple mildly anticholinergic drugs cause cognitive impairment, falls, and delirium
mcqs.explanation: Multiple medications with mild anticholinergic properties produce cumulative burden causing confusion, falls, urinary retention, constipation, and delirium.
mcqs.q: Anticholinergic burden in elderly is problematic because:
mcqs.correct: Cumulative effects from multiple mildly anticholinergic drugs cause cognitive impairment, falls, and delirium

description: Polypharmacy (≥5 medications) affects >40% of elderly. Beers Criteria and STOPP/START guide inappropriate prescribing. Key concepts: prescribing cascade, anticholinergic burden, drug-drug interactions. Deprescribing: one medication at a time with monitoring. Always consider renal/hepatic dose adjustments.

flashcards.q: מה הטיפול הראשון באי שליטה בשתן?
flashcards.a: הערכת גורמים הפיכים, timed voiding, אימון רצפת האגן.
שילוב גריאטר/ית, פיזיותרפיה, סיעוד להנחיה ותמיכה.

flashcards.back: Stress (cough/sneeze), Urge (overactive bladder), Overflow (retention), Functional (can't reach toilet), Mixed (stress+urge). DIAPPERS mnemonic for transient causes.
Kegel exercises (pelvic floor training) = first-line for stress and mixed UI (Cochrane SOE=A). 'Freeze and squeeze' technique for urge. Contract pelvic floor before activities causing leakage.

flashcards.front: Oral vs vaginal estrogen for incontinence
flashcards.back: ORAL conjugated estrogen WORSENS incontinence (SOE=A) — never use. VAGINAL topical estrogen cream may be helpful adjunct for atrophic changes.
Requires measurement of serum creatinine and referral to urology (SOE=C). May need intermittent self-catheterization. Do NOT start anticholinergics without checking PVR.
Cholinesterase inhibitors have procholinergic effects → increase detrusor contractility → urinary urgency and urge incontinence. Population studies confirm (SOE=A).
Patient stands, coughs vigorously. Immediate leakage = highly specific for STRESS incontinence (SOE=A). Delayed leakage suggests urge component.
Large capacity, poor contraction, no obstruction on urodynamics. PVR very high (>300-650 mL). Treatment: intermittent self-catheterization, NOT TURP or anticholinergics.

flashcards.front: Medications causing urinary retention in elderly
flashcards.back: Anticholinergics (diphenhydramine), alpha-agonist decongestants, opioids, TCAs, calcium channel blockers. Always review med list for new incontinence or retention.

mcqs.q: מה הטיפול הראשוני?
mcqs.explanation: גישה לא‑כירורגית ראשונית.
Behavioral therapy with Kegels is first-line for stress and mixed incontinence (SOE=A for Kegels in Cochrane review). Oral estrogen WORSENS incontinence. Surgery is not first-line.
mcqs.q: A 72-year-old woman has stress and urge urinary incontinence for 3 years. Pelvic exam shows strong pelvic floor contraction. Best initial management?

options: Standing still and contracting pelvic floor muscles until urgency passes
Taking an extra dose of anticholinergic medication
Applying cold compress to the suprapubic area
mcqs.explanation: Patients should stay very still, repeatedly contract pelvic floor muscles until urgency resolves, then proceed to bathroom. If urgency returns en route, repeat the technique.
mcqs.q: The 'freeze and squeeze' technique teaches patients to manage urgency by:
mcqs.correct: Standing still and contracting pelvic floor muscles until urgency passes

mcqs.explanation: PVR >300 mL requires urologic referral (SOE=C). Urodynamics show neurogenic bladder (poor contractility, no obstruction). Self-catheterization preserves upper tract. TURP useless without obstruction. Oxybutynin could worsen retention.
mcqs.q: A 70-year-old man with diabetes has urinary urgency/frequency. Postvoid residual is 650 mL. Urodynamics show large capacity, poor contraction, no obstruction. Best management?

options: Improve urge but not stress incontinence
mcqs.explanation: Oral conjugated estrogen therapy has been shown to WORSEN urinary incontinence and should not be used (SOE=A). Vaginal topical estrogen cream might be a helpful adjunct.
mcqs.q: Oral conjugated estrogen therapy in elderly women with urinary incontinence has been shown to:

mcqs.explanation: Chronic catheters cause UTI, obstruction, urethral damage, strictures, bladder stones, and bladder spasm. They should not be the initial recommendation for incontinence.
mcqs.q: Chronic indwelling urinary catheter complications include all EXCEPT:

mcqs.explanation: A positive standing cough test (immediate urine leakage with cough) is a highly specific sign for stress incontinence (SOE=A).
mcqs.q: A positive standing cough test in a woman with urinary symptoms is highly specific for:

options: It confirms the diagnosis of BPH
Anticholinergics could cause complete retention if PVR is already elevated
Normal PVR excludes the need for treatment
PVR determines the choice of anticholinergic
mcqs.explanation: Oxybutynin could result in complete urine retention in a patient with untreated elevated PVR (SOE=A). Always check PVR before adding anticholinergics in men with BPH.
mcqs.q: In a man with BPH symptoms, checking postvoid residual is important before starting anticholinergics because:
mcqs.correct: Anticholinergics could cause complete retention if PVR is already elevated

options: Anticholinergic inhibition of bladder smooth muscle contraction
mcqs.explanation: Diphenhydramine's anticholinergic effects inhibit contraction of bladder smooth muscle (detrusor), causing urinary retention. A normal PVR would exclude this as the cause.
mcqs.q: Diphenhydramine can cause urinary retention by:
mcqs.correct: Anticholinergic inhibition of bladder smooth muscle contraction

options: Only if pyuria is present on urinalysis
Never, in the absence of acute symptoms
Only if the culture shows >100,000 CFU/mL
mcqs.explanation: In stable chronic incontinence without acute symptoms, and with a clean urinalysis, a positive culture likely represents contamination or asymptomatic bacteriuria. Treatment would not improve incontinence (SOE=A).
mcqs.q: Asymptomatic bacteriuria in an elderly woman with stable chronic urinary incontinence should be treated with antibiotics:
mcqs.correct: Never, in the absence of acute symptoms

mcqs.explanation: Procholinergic drugs (donepezil) increase bladder smooth muscle contractility → urgency and urge incontinence. Population studies confirm this association (SOE=A).
mcqs.q: An 84-year-old on donepezil develops urinary urgency. Discontinuing donepezil would most likely improve incontinence because:

mcqs.explanation: RLS causes severe sleep disruption with frequent awakenings, contributing to nocturia. Diagnosis: uncomfortable leg sensations at rest, circadian pattern, relieved by movement.
mcqs.q: Restless legs syndrome can contribute to urinary symptoms primarily through:

mcqs.explanation: Pramipexole (dopamine agonist) is first-line for restless legs syndrome which can indirectly contribute to nocturia through sleep disruption.
mcqs.q: Pramipexole is first-line treatment for:

options: Alpha-blocker therapy has been maximized and voiding symptoms persist
mcqs.explanation: Adding anticholinergic bladder medications to alpha-blocker may improve incontinence in BPH with overactive bladder (SOE=A), but only after alpha-blocker is maximized.
mcqs.q: Tolterodine added to alpha-blocker therapy in men with BPH is appropriate when:
mcqs.correct: Alpha-blocker therapy has been maximized and voiding symptoms persist

options: Lower risk of UTI, stones, and urethral damage
mcqs.explanation: Indwelling catheters cause UTI, obstruction, urethral damage, strictures, bladder stones, and spasm. Self-catheterization preserves upper tract with lower complication rates.
mcqs.q: Intermittent self-catheterization is preferred over indwelling catheter because:
mcqs.correct: Lower risk of UTI, stones, and urethral damage

mcqs.explanation: Large capacity + poor contraction + no obstruction = poorly contractile/neurogenic bladder. Common in diabetic neuropathy. Self-catheterization is the appropriate treatment.
mcqs.q: Urodynamic testing showing large capacity, poor contraction, and no obstruction suggests:

options: May be a helpful adjunct, unlike oral estrogen which worsens incontinence
mcqs.explanation: Key distinction: ORAL conjugated estrogen worsens incontinence (SOE=A). VAGINAL topical estrogen cream may be a helpful adjunct for atrophic changes.
mcqs.q: Vaginal topical estrogen in urinary incontinence:
mcqs.correct: May be a helpful adjunct, unlike oral estrogen which worsens incontinence

options: Stress incontinence (positive = immediate leakage with cough)
mcqs.explanation: Patient stands, coughs vigorously — immediate urine leakage is highly specific for stress incontinence (SOE=A). Distinguished from urge incontinence by timing.
mcqs.q: The standing cough test assesses for:
mcqs.correct: Stress incontinence (positive = immediate leakage with cough)

options: Inability to reach toilet due to mobility, restraints, or cognitive impairment
mcqs.explanation: Functional incontinence: intact urinary tract but inability to toilet due to immobility, restraints, delirium, or environmental barriers. Addressed by removing barriers.
mcqs.q: Functional incontinence in hospitalized elderly is often caused by:
mcqs.correct: Inability to reach toilet due to mobility, restraints, or cognitive impairment

options: All medications with anticholinergic, diuretic, or autonomic effects
mcqs.explanation: Multiple drug classes affect continence: anticholinergics (retention), cholinesterase inhibitors (urgency), diuretics (frequency), alpha-blockers (relaxation), sedatives (functional).
mcqs.q: In an elderly patient with new incontinence, medication review should focus on:
mcqs.correct: All medications with anticholinergic, diuretic, or autonomic effects

description: Urinary incontinence affects 15-30% of community-dwelling elderly. Types: stress, urge, overflow, functional, mixed. Evaluation: history, PVR measurement, urinalysis. First-line: behavioral therapy, Kegel exercises, bladder training. Avoid anticholinergics in cognitive impairment. Oral estrogen worsens incontinence.

flashcards.a: אימון התנגדות + חלבון עשיר בליאוצין.
1.0–1.2 g/kg חלבון, ליאוצין, ויטמין D.

flashcards.back: Progressive loss of muscle mass AND strength/function with aging. Screen: SARC-F questionnaire (≥4 positive). Confirm: grip strength, gait speed, muscle mass (DEXA/BIA).
Crucial essential amino acid for combating sarcopenia (SOE=B). Long-term essential amino acid intake recognized as important for prevention and treatment.
Validated nutritional screening tool for elderly. Screens body weight, weight loss, dietary intake, disease burden. Preferred over albumin/prealbumin which reflect inflammation, not nutrition.

flashcards.front: Why NOT albumin for nutritional assessment?
flashcards.back: Inflammation and injury alter albumin/prealbumin synthesis and degradation, limiting usefulness for nutritional assessment specifically. They correlate with outcomes but not nutrition status.
Standard: Osmolite 1.0 kcal/mL. Volume-restricted: TwoCal HN 2.0. ESRD: Nepro (low K+). Diabetes: Glucerna. High protein: Promote. Fat malabsorption: Vivonex.
97% sensitivity, 90% specificity for dysphagia (SOE=B). MUST be done before ANY feeding. Trial of feeding without assessment increases aspiration risk (SOE=A).
Olive oil, vegetables, nuts, fruits. Associated with CVD prevention, metabolic syndrome reversal. Antioxidants and fiber may reduce transient oxidative stress from food intake.
ASCO first-line recommendation. 2.5 mg bedtime: 60% gained ≥5% weight vs 9% placebo (p<.001). Mirtazapine ineffective in large RCT. Megestrol/steroids increase appetite but don't improve survival.

mcqs.q: מה לא טיפול טיפוסי?
mcqs.explanation: השילוב עם פעילות נחוץ.
DHEA, testosterone (SOE=B), leucine, and vitamin D all improve muscle mass. Vitamins C and E (antioxidants) have only shown promise in animal studies, not in humans.
mcqs.q: Which supplement has NOT been shown to improve human skeletal muscle mass?

options: Weight loss by dietary management alone
Combined weight loss and regular exercise
Physical therapy for gait and balance only
mcqs.explanation: Obesity with low muscle mass relative to body weight (relative sarcopenia) is best treated with combined weight loss (~10%) and exercise. Either alone is less effective (SOE=A).
mcqs.q: The most effective management for an obese older adult with functional limitation due to relative sarcopenia is:
mcqs.correct: Combined weight loss and regular exercise

options: Body weight, weight loss, and tools like MNA or MST
mcqs.explanation: Screening should include body weight, weight loss assessment, and validated tools like the Mini Nutritional Assessment (MNA) or Malnutrition Screening Tool (MST). Albumin/prealbumin are affected by inflammation and do not accurately reflect nutritional status.
mcqs.q: Screening for nutritional status in older adults should routinely include:
mcqs.correct: Body weight, weight loss, and tools like MNA or MST

options: Inflammation and injury alter their synthesis and degradation
They only reflect visceral protein status
They are not available in routine labs
mcqs.explanation: Although albumin and prealbumin correlate with clinical outcomes, inflammation and injury alter their synthesis and degradation, limiting their usefulness for nutritional assessment specifically.
mcqs.q: Plasma albumin and prealbumin should NOT be used to assess malnutrition because:
mcqs.correct: Inflammation and injury alter their synthesis and degradation

options: Vitamin D receptors in skeletal muscle important for protein synthesis
mcqs.explanation: Vitamin D receptors are present in skeletal muscle and important for protein synthesis. Proximal muscle weakness is a sign of osteomalacia. USPSTF meta-analysis showed 17% fall reduction with vitamin D.
mcqs.q: Vitamin D's role in sarcopenia prevention is related to:
mcqs.correct: Vitamin D receptors in skeletal muscle important for protein synthesis

options: Do not decrease with age; protein should be a greater proportion of diet
Remain exactly the same per kg/day
Should be restricted to protect kidney function
mcqs.explanation: Protein requirements do not decrease with age (SOE=C). Since total energy requirements decline, protein should make up a proportionally greater part of the diet in older adults.
mcqs.q: Protein requirements in older adults compared to younger adults:
mcqs.correct: Do not decrease with age; protein should be a greater proportion of diet

mcqs.explanation: Stroke guidelines recommend swallowing assessment before any feeding. A standardized bedside swallowing test detects post-stroke dysphagia with 97% sensitivity and 90% specificity (SOE=B).
mcqs.q: After a left hemispheric stroke with moderate hemiparesis, the most appropriate initial step before feeding is:

options: Increasing dietary salt restriction for all patients
mcqs.explanation: Unnecessarily restricted diets (limiting salt, fat, sugar) can contribute to inadequate eating. Other barriers include polypharmacy, dental health, food insecurity, depression, and mealtime isolation.
mcqs.q: Prevention of malnutrition in older adults requires addressing all EXCEPT:
mcqs.correct: Increasing dietary salt restriction for all patients

options: Prevention of CVD progression and reversal of metabolic syndrome
No proven benefits over standard Western diet
mcqs.explanation: The traditional Mediterranean diet (olive oil, vegetables, nuts, fruits) meets healthy diet criteria and is associated with prevention of CVD progression and reversal of metabolic syndrome.
mcqs.q: The Mediterranean diet in older adults is associated with:
mcqs.correct: Prevention of CVD progression and reversal of metabolic syndrome

options: Strength, Assistance walking, Rise from chair, Climb stairs, Falls
mcqs.explanation: SARC-F: 5-item self-report screening for sarcopenia. Score ≥4 suggests sarcopenia requiring further assessment with grip strength and gait speed.
mcqs.q: The SARC-F screening questionnaire assesses:
mcqs.correct: Strength, Assistance walking, Rise from chair, Climb stairs, Falls

options: At least 1.0-1.2 g/kg/day, a greater proportion of total calories
Same as young adults at 0.8 g/kg/day
mcqs.explanation: Requirements don't decrease with age. Since total energy needs decline, protein should be proportionally greater. General consensus ≥1.0-1.2 g/kg/day (SOE=C).
mcqs.q: Protein requirements in older adults should be:
mcqs.correct: At least 1.0-1.2 g/kg/day, a greater proportion of total calories

mcqs.explanation: Enteral feeding limits mucosal atrophy, maintains IgA secretion, prevents cholelithiasis, costs less, and has fewer infectious complications. Speed of delivery is not an advantage.
mcqs.q: Enteral feeding is preferred over parenteral because of all EXCEPT:

mcqs.explanation: TwoCal HN at 2.0 kcal/mL provides maximum calories in minimum volume for fluid-restricted patients. Standard formulas at 1.0 kcal/mL require more volume.
mcqs.q: For a volume-restricted patient needing tube feeding, which formula is most appropriate?

mcqs.explanation: Nepro: 1.8 kcal/mL, 48% lipid, low potassium (27.2 mEq/L), designed for ESRD patients on dialysis with restricted potassium and fluid needs.
mcqs.q: Nepro with Carb Steady formula is designed for patients with:

options: Contribute to inadequate eating and malnutrition
mcqs.explanation: Overly restricted diets contribute to malnutrition in elderly. Prevention requires addressing these along with polypharmacy, dental health, depression, and mealtime isolation.
mcqs.q: Unnecessary dietary restrictions in elderly (low salt, low fat, low sugar) can:
mcqs.correct: Contribute to inadequate eating and malnutrition

mcqs.explanation: Regular moderate exercise delays CAD, diabetes, osteoporosis, obesity, cognitive impairment; protects against functional decline; improves mood. It doesn't prevent chromosomal mutations.
mcqs.q: Physical activity in elderly protects against all EXCEPT:

mcqs.explanation: At least 30 minutes moderate-intensity physical activity on most, preferably all, days. Even moderate activity substantially drops all-cause mortality.
mcqs.q: The recommended daily physical activity for older adults is:

options: It responds well to caloric replacement
It cannot be reversed by simply replacing calories and nutrients
It only occurs in pancreatic cancer
It improves with appetite stimulants alone
mcqs.explanation: Cancer cachexia is multifactorial (metabolic dysregulation, inflammation, muscle wasting) and cannot be reversed by caloric replacement. Olanzapine is recommended first-line for anorexia.
mcqs.q: Cancer cachexia differs from simple malnutrition in that:
mcqs.correct: It cannot be reversed by simply replacing calories and nutrients

mcqs.explanation: In 124 solid tumor patients, 60% on olanzapine gained ≥5% weight vs 9% placebo (p<.001). Recommended as first-line by ASCO guidelines. Mirtazapine was ineffective.
mcqs.q: Olanzapine for cancer-related anorexia at 2.5 mg bedtime showed what result?

description: Sarcopenia: progressive loss of muscle mass and strength with aging. Screening: SARC-F, grip strength, gait speed. Risk factors: inactivity, malnutrition, inflammation. Treatment: resistance exercise + adequate protein (1.0-1.2 g/kg/day), vitamin D, leucine. MNA for nutritional screening. Mediterranean diet protective.

flashcards.a: ניהול תסמינים, נוחות, שיח עם משפחה.
Advance Care Planning – העדפות מטופל.

flashcards.back: Process supporting adults in understanding and sharing personal values, life goals, and preferences regarding future medical care during serious/chronic illness.

flashcards.front: Goals of care vs advance directives
flashcards.back: Goals: patient-directed wishes for wellbeing/accomplishments, often non-medical, spanning multiple diseases. Advance directives: wishes for care during serious acute illness + healthcare proxy appointment.
DNR applies ONLY to cardiac/respiratory arrest. Does NOT apply to other decisions (dialysis, surgery, antibiotics). Always assess capacity for current decision at hand.
Greater satisfaction with quality of dying, better quality of care, less conflict in decisions. Caregivers: more effort but NO increase in depression, anxiety, or persistent grief.
New chemo within 14 days of death, ICU in last month, >1 ED visit last month, >14 days hospitalization, hospice stay <3 days. Nearly 20% of cancer patients got chemo in last 2 weeks.
DSM-5: 6-12 months after death. Affects 9-25% of elderly bereaved. Associated with disability, cognitive impairment, suicidal thinking. Targeted psychotherapy > standard interpersonal therapy.
POLST: physician orders immediately actionable, travels with patient. Standard AD: broader values document, may need interpretation. POLST reduces aggressive treatment in nursing homes.

flashcards.front: Cultural competence at end of life
flashcards.back: For many Black Americans: limited trust in healthcare, longevity may trump quality of life, advance directives perceived as denial of care. Always ask about decision-making approaches and preferences.

mcqs.q: מה הגישה הנכונה?
mcqs.explanation: טיפול פליאטיבי ממוקד.

options: Assess her capacity to decide about hemodialysis
mcqs.explanation: DNR applies to cardiac/respiratory arrest only, not dialysis decisions. Since the patient is alert, assess her decision-making capacity first. She may be able to give or refuse informed consent.
mcqs.q: An 88-year-old with DNR, bilateral amputations develops acute renal failure requiring dialysis. She is alert. The most appropriate next step is:
mcqs.correct: Assess her capacity to decide about hemodialysis

options: Reasoning in context of personal values
mcqs.explanation: Capacity requires understanding, reasoning, appreciation of consequences, and communication of choice. The patient need NOT agree with the physician - competent patients may refuse recommended treatment.
mcqs.q: Decision-making capacity requires all EXCEPT:

options: Document refusal and proceed with discharge
Arrange patient/family meeting to discuss wishes and preferences
Insist on completing directives before discharge
mcqs.explanation: Cultural competence is essential. Many Black Americans have limited trust in healthcare systems and may perceive advance directives negatively. A collaborative meeting to understand the patient's and family's values and care preferences is most appropriate.
mcqs.q: An 86-year-old Black man refuses to complete advance directives. The most appropriate approach is:
mcqs.correct: Arrange patient/family meeting to discuss wishes and preferences

options: Wishes for care in the context of serious acute illness
mcqs.explanation: Goals of care are patient-directed wishes for wellbeing (often non-medical). Advance directives focus on wishes for care during serious illness, including appointment of a healthcare proxy.
mcqs.q: Advance care planning differs from goals of care in that advance directives focus on:
mcqs.correct: Wishes for care in the context of serious acute illness

mcqs.explanation: Annual mortality is 8-10% for ages 85+ and over 30% for ages 90+. This highlights the importance of clarifying end-of-life preferences as a priority for the very old.
mcqs.q: The risk of death per year in people aged 85+ is approximately:

options: Greater satisfaction with quality of dying
mcqs.explanation: Hospice involves greater caregiver effort (medication administration, time off work), but caregivers do NOT report more depression, anxiety, or persistent grief — hospice buffers the negative impact.
mcqs.q: Hospice use is associated with all EXCEPT:

mcqs.explanation: DSM-5 defines complex bereavement disorder as lasting 6-12 months after death. In older adults, it's associated with disability, cognitive impairment, and suicidal thinking. Affects 9-25% of bereaved older adults.
mcqs.q: Persistent/complicated grief in older adults is defined as lasting beyond:

options: Bolster sense of meaning and reduce existential distress
mcqs.explanation: Dignity therapy is a short-term psychotherapy that improves well-being in people with life-limiting illness by supporting meaning-making. Patients with higher sense of meaning experience less end-of-life distress.
mcqs.q: Dignity therapy at end of life is designed to:
mcqs.correct: Bolster sense of meaning and reduce existential distress

options: New chemotherapy within 14 days of death
ICU admission in last month of life
Multiple ED visits in last month
mcqs.explanation: Timely hospice enrollment (>7 days) is considered appropriate care. Aggressive markers include late chemotherapy, short hospice stays (<3 days), multiple ED visits, >14 days hospitalization, and ICU admission in the last month.
mcqs.q: Aggressive treatment at end of life in older adults with cancer includes all EXCEPT:

options: Sharing personal values, life goals, and preferences for future care
mcqs.explanation: 2017 consensus: ACP supports adults in understanding and sharing personal values, life goals, and preferences regarding future medical care during serious illness.
mcqs.q: Advance care planning consensus definition focuses on:
mcqs.correct: Sharing personal values, life goals, and preferences for future care

options: Are overarching, often non-medical, spanning multiple diseases
mcqs.explanation: Goals of care are patient-directed wishes for wellbeing (e.g., attending grandchild's wedding, sleeping without pain). They span diseases and are often non-medical. Advance directives focus on acute illness decisions.
mcqs.q: Goals of care differ from advance directives in that goals:
mcqs.correct: Are overarching, often non-medical, spanning multiple diseases

mcqs.explanation: 8-10% per year for ages 85+, and OVER 30% per year for ages 90+. This highlights urgency of clarifying end-of-life preferences in the very old.
mcqs.q: Annual mortality rate for people aged 90+ is approximately:

mcqs.explanation: Older adults are more likely to initiate such discussions than their adult children. Adult children may resist and often favor more aggressive treatment than parents.
mcqs.q: Who is more likely to initiate end-of-life discussions?

options: Less aggressive treatment at end of life
mcqs.explanation: Older adults completing POLST are less likely to receive aggressive treatment in nursing homes, though this effect was not seen in ER admissions.
mcqs.q: POLST completion in nursing home residents is associated with:
mcqs.correct: Less aggressive treatment at end of life

options: 99497 and 99498, voluntarily, with no completed document required
mcqs.explanation: Since 2016, voluntary ACP discussions reimbursed under 99497/99498. No completed document required. Can bill multiple encounters. But only 1.6% of Medicare Advantage patients had a billed discussion.
mcqs.q: Medicare reimburses advance care planning discussions under CPT codes:
mcqs.correct: 99497 and 99498, voluntarily, with no completed document required

options: Never appropriate while patient is alive
An emerging priority to reduce pill burden
mcqs.explanation: Deprescribing medications no longer appropriate is increasingly recognized as important at end of life, though defining best practices remains challenging.
mcqs.q: Deprescribing medications at end of life is:
mcqs.correct: An emerging priority to reduce pill burden

options: Higher rates of depression and anxiety
No increase in depression/anxiety despite greater caregiving involvement
mcqs.explanation: Despite greater involvement (medications, time off work), hospice caregivers do NOT report more depression, anxiety, or persistent grief. Hospice buffers negative caregiving impact.
mcqs.q: Caregiver bereavement after hospice use shows:
mcqs.correct: No increase in depression/anxiety despite greater caregiving involvement

options: Breaking bad news to patients and families
mcqs.explanation: SPIKES: Setting, Perception, Invitation, Knowledge, Empathy, Strategy/Summary. Structured framework for delivering difficult news in clinical settings.
mcqs.q: The SPIKES protocol is used for:
mcqs.correct: Breaking bad news to patients and families

mcqs.explanation: SEER analysis of 30,000 patients aged 65+ showed nearly 20% received chemotherapy in last 2 weeks of life, reflecting persistent aggressive end-of-life treatment.
mcqs.q: Nearly what percentage of cancer patients continued chemotherapy in the last 2 weeks of life?

description: End-of-life care encompasses advance care planning, goals of care discussions, hospice, and palliative care. Key documents: advance directives, POLST, healthcare proxy. Hospice for prognosis ≤6 months. Annual mortality 8-10% for ages 85+. Dignity therapy for existential distress. Cultural competence essential.

flashcards.a: טיפול אנטי‑טרומבוטי, שליטה ב‑BP, שיקום מוקדם.

flashcards.back: C=CHF(1), H=HTN(1), A₂=Age≥75(2), D=DM(1), S₂=Stroke/TIA(2), V=Vascular disease(1), A=Age 65-74(1), Sc=Sex female(1). Max 9. Score ≥2 in males or ≥3 in females → anticoagulate.
Bedside test: 97% sensitivity, 90% specificity. MUST do before ANY oral feeding. Feeding without assessment increases aspiration risk (SOE=A). Then videofluoroscopy if needed.
Multiple functional deficits (hemiparesis, ADL dependence, aphasia), cognitively able to participate, engaged in therapy, follows commands. Needs comprehensive PT + OT + speech.
Depression is reversible cause of functional decline post-stroke. Screen with PHQ-2 → PHQ-9. Significant barrier to rehabilitation participation and recovery.
Common in elderly stroke patients. Increases LOS, cognitive/functional decline, post-hospital depression. Reassess cognition weeks later — may unmask pre-existing dementia.
Brief, accurate, acceptable to ED/ward providers. Doesn't require extensive training like full CAM. Components: alertness, AMT4, attention, acute change.
Duplex ultrasound, CTA, MRA provide adequate diagnostic accuracy vs intra-arterial angiography for symptomatic carotid stenosis (meta-analysis).
Diabetes independently increases fall risk: neuropathy, hypoglycemia, orthostatic hypotension. Must provide fall risk education and PT-guided mobility program.
1) List days of the week backward, 2) What day is it today? Quick bedside screen for hospitalized patients. Positive result warrants full cognitive assessment.

mcqs.q: מה חשוב למניעת שבץ חוזר?
mcqs.explanation: מפחית סיכון לחזרה.

options: Continue nursing home physical therapy only
mcqs.explanation: She needs comprehensive inpatient rehabilitation — she is engaged in therapy, follows commands, and has multiple functional deficits requiring PT, OT, and speech therapy. The nursing home alone is insufficient.
mcqs.q: A 75-year-old woman had a stroke 2 weeks ago with right hemiparesis and mild aphasia. She was delirious from UTI but has recovered cognitively. She is dependent in transfers, toileting, and IADLs. Best next step?

mcqs.explanation: Stroke guidelines mandate swallowing assessment before any feeding. Bedside test has 97% sensitivity and 90% specificity for dysphagia (SOE=B). Feeding without assessment increases aspiration risk (SOE=A).
mcqs.q: Before beginning any oral feeding after stroke, the first step should be:

options: When there are functional deficits in ADLs, communication, or cognition
mcqs.explanation: Comprehensive rehabilitation should include all relevant disciplines based on the patient's deficits. This patient with hemiparesis, aphasia, and ADL dependence needs PT, OT, and speech therapy.
mcqs.q: For stroke patients, occupational therapy, speech therapy, and language pathology consultations are indicated when:
mcqs.correct: When there are functional deficits in ADLs, communication, or cognition

mcqs.explanation: Diabetes increases fall risk in older adults. Clinicians must be attentive to fall risk education and offer mobility programs guided by physical therapy.
mcqs.q: Diabetes mellitus increases the risk of which post-stroke complication that requires specific education?

mcqs.explanation: Patients who develop delirium should have cognition reassessed several weeks later, after the acute confusional state resolves, to determine true baseline cognitive function.
mcqs.q: Delirium after stroke should prompt reassessment of cognition:

mcqs.explanation: CHA₂DS₂-VASc stratifies stroke risk in atrial fibrillation to guide anticoagulation decisions. Components: CHF, HTN, Age≥75 (2pts), DM, prior Stroke/TIA (2pts), Vascular disease, Age 65-74, Sex (female).
mcqs.q: CHA₂DS₂-VASc scoring is used in stroke prevention for:

options: Delirium — acute change, inattention, disorganized thinking, altered consciousness
mcqs.explanation: CAM evaluates delirium by assessing acute change/fluctuating course, inattention, disorganized thinking, and altered level of consciousness. It has an ICU version.
mcqs.q: In a post-stroke patient, the Confusion Assessment Method (CAM) assesses:
mcqs.correct: Delirium — acute change, inattention, disorganized thinking, altered consciousness

mcqs.explanation: Depression is a reversible cause of functional decline post-stroke. It should be screened for and treated, as it significantly impairs rehabilitation participation and recovery.
mcqs.q: Functional decline after stroke may be reversible when caused by:

options: Draw a clock and name 3 animals
List days of week backward and state current day
mcqs.explanation: The Ultra Brief two-item screen asks patients to list the days of the week backward and identify the current day. It's useful for quick bedside cognitive assessment in hospitalized patients.
mcqs.q: The Ultra Brief bedside cognitive screen asks patients to:
mcqs.correct: List days of week backward and state current day

mcqs.explanation: 2-point items: Age ≥75 and prior Stroke/TIA/thromboembolism. 1-point: CHF, HTN, DM, vascular disease, age 65-74, female sex. Max score 9.
mcqs.q: CHA₂DS₂-VASc score components giving 2 points each are:

mcqs.explanation: Bedside swallowing test: 97% sensitivity, 90% specificity for dysphagia after stroke (SOE=B). Must be done before ANY oral feeding.
mcqs.q: A standardized bedside swallowing test detects post-stroke dysphagia with:

mcqs.explanation: CAM-ICU version assesses delirium in ICU settings where patients may be intubated or sedated. Important for post-stroke patients in critical care.
mcqs.q: The CAM-ICU is specifically designed to assess delirium in:

options: A 'stress test for the brain' unmasking decreased cerebral reserve
An expected surgical complication requiring no evaluation
A sign of medication overuse only
mcqs.explanation: Delirium as 'stress test for the brain' — infection/drugs unmask decreased cerebral reserve and may herald underlying neurodegenerative disease requiring outpatient screening.
mcqs.q: Delirium in a post-stroke patient can be viewed as:
mcqs.correct: A 'stress test for the brain' unmasking decreased cerebral reserve

options: Patient has deficits in ADLs, mobility, AND communication
mcqs.explanation: Comprehensive rehabilitation addressing all deficits. This patient with hemiparesis (PT), ADL dependence (OT), and aphasia (speech) needs all three simultaneously.
mcqs.q: Post-stroke rehabilitation should include OT, PT, and speech therapy when:
mcqs.correct: Patient has deficits in ADLs, mobility, AND communication

options: The most common form in elderly, often missed
Easy to diagnose without screening tools
mcqs.explanation: Hypoactive delirium is most common in elderly (slowed movement, paucity of speech, unresponsiveness) but identified far less frequently than hyperactive form by staff.
mcqs.q: Hypoactive delirium in hospitalized stroke patients is:
mcqs.correct: The most common form in elderly, often missed

options: DM increases fall risk independently through neuropathy and hypoglycemia
Diabetes causes blindness in all patients
mcqs.explanation: DM independently increases fall risk via peripheral neuropathy, hypoglycemia, and orthostatic hypotension. Clinicians must provide fall risk education and mobility programs.
mcqs.q: A stroke patient with diabetes has increased risk of falls because:
mcqs.correct: DM increases fall risk independently through neuropathy and hypoglycemia

options: Several weeks later to determine true baseline
Never — delirium has no cognitive implications
mcqs.explanation: Delirium may unmask previously unrecognized dementia. Reassess cognition several weeks after resolution to determine true baseline and screen for underlying neurodegenerative disease.
mcqs.q: After delirium resolves in a hospitalized patient, cognition should be reassessed:
mcqs.correct: Several weeks later to determine true baseline

options: Balances brevity, accuracy, and emergency provider acceptability
mcqs.explanation: 4AT, along with bCAM and RASS, more favorably balance brevity, accuracy, and ED provider acceptability compared to full CAM which requires careful training.
mcqs.q: The 4AT delirium screening tool advantages in emergency settings include:
mcqs.correct: Balances brevity, accuracy, and emergency provider acceptability

options: Provides adequate diagnostic accuracy for most clinical decisions
Is only useful for screening, not treatment decisions
mcqs.explanation: Meta-analysis showed non-invasive imaging (duplex ultrasound, CTA, MRA) provides adequate diagnostic accuracy for symptomatic carotid stenosis evaluation.
mcqs.q: Carotid stenosis evaluation by non-invasive imaging compared to intra-arterial angiography:
mcqs.correct: Provides adequate diagnostic accuracy for most clinical decisions

description: Stroke is a leading cause of disability in elderly. Assessment: NIHSS for severity, CHA₂DS₂-VASc for AF-related stroke risk. Swallowing assessment mandatory before feeding. Comprehensive rehabilitation (PT/OT/speech). Screen for post-stroke depression. Delirium common — reassess cognition weeks later.

topic: אי ספיקת לב / יתר לחץ דם

flashcards.a: שליטה בלחץ דם, תרופות מותאמות, ניטור נוזלים ותפקוד.

flashcards.back: Echocardiography: assesses ventricular function, valvular disease, wall motion, hypertrophy. CXR and ECG alone cannot determine specific cardiac abnormality.

flashcards.front: Medications to AVOID in elderly HF
flashcards.back: NSAIDs (Na/H2O retention), thiazolidinediones (fluid retention), non-dihydropyridine CCBs (negative inotrope in systolic HF), most antiarrhythmics except amiodarone.
BP lying → standing at 1 and 3 minutes. Positive: SBP drop ≥20 or DBP drop ≥10. High-yield exam in elderly on HF meds. Predicts falls, syncope, fractures.
Upside risk: bonus for quality, no loss liability. Downside risk: must repay losses if quality benchmark not met. Stronger incentive for high-value care but more financial uncertainty.

RN + 2-5 physicians in primary care. Home assessment, care/action plans, monthly monitoring, self-care promotion, transitional care, caregiver support, community resources. RCT showed improved quality ratings.

flashcards.front: Depression in HF — prevalence and impact
flashcards.back: Up to 40% prevalence. Worsens adherence, hospitalizations, mortality, quality of life. Screen with PHQ-2 → PHQ-9. Treatable — collaborative care models effective.
Cornerstone therapy. Start low, go slow. Monitor K+ and creatinine. Not automatically contraindicated with CKD. Cough is class effect (switch to ARB). Angioedema rare but serious.
Adults 65+ = >30% of observation admissions. Alternative to hospital admission: evaluate therapeutic response, additional testing, facilitate care transitions.
Focus on syndrome detection (delirium, falls, frailty, polypharmacy), not just disease. EQUiPPED for med safety. Interdisciplinary staffing. Observation units as admission alternative.

mcqs.q: גישה לטיפול ב‑HTN?
mcqs.explanation: איזון עדין עם ניטור.

options: Arrange patient/family meeting to discuss wishes and options
mcqs.explanation: Respectful discussion of the patient's and family's values, decision-making approaches, and care preferences should guide the collaborative treatment plan.
mcqs.q: An 86-year-old with class IV heart failure, diabetes, CKD, and DNR is ready for discharge. The patient and wife refuse rehab facility. Most appropriate step?
mcqs.correct: Arrange patient/family meeting to discuss wishes and options

mcqs.explanation: NSAIDs promote sodium and water retention and can worsen heart failure. They should be avoided in elderly patients with HF. ACE inhibitors and beta-blockers are actually beneficial in HF.
mcqs.q: In an elderly patient with heart failure, which medication class from Beers Criteria requires particular caution?

options: Orthostatic hypotension predisposing to falls and syncope
mcqs.explanation: Postural blood pressure is a high-yield, often non-traditional exam element that detects orthostatic hypotension — common in elderly patients on antihypertensives, diuretics, or with autonomic dysfunction.
mcqs.q: Postural blood pressure measurement is a high-yield examination element in older adults because it can detect:
mcqs.correct: Orthostatic hypotension predisposing to falls and syncope

options: It is a summary measure of overall health impact and guides care planning
It is required for insurance billing
mcqs.explanation: Functional status is a summary measure of the overall impact of health conditions. Changes in functional status should prompt diagnostic evaluation. It drives care planning for older adults.
mcqs.q: Functional status measurement in older heart failure patients is essential because:
mcqs.correct: It is a summary measure of overall health impact and guides care planning

options: Class IV symptoms despite optimal therapy
mcqs.explanation: Hospice requires a prognosis of ≤6 months. Criteria for HF include refractory Class IV symptoms, recurrent hospitalizations, and poor prognosis despite optimal medical therapy.
mcqs.q: Hospice eligibility for end-stage heart failure includes all EXCEPT:

options: A marker of aggressive end-of-life treatment
mcqs.explanation: ICU use in the last month of life is a marker of aggressive end-of-life care. About 30% of Medicare beneficiaries have ICU admissions in their final month. This rate is declining slowly.
mcqs.q: ICU admission during the last month of life in elderly heart failure patients is considered:
mcqs.correct: A marker of aggressive end-of-life treatment

options: Achieving JNC targets as quickly as possible
mcqs.explanation: In frail elderly patients, aggressive BP lowering risks orthostatic hypotension, falls, and fractures. Treatment should be individualized with careful monitoring of standing BP.
mcqs.q: When treating hypertension in frail elderly patients, the most important consideration is:

options: PHQ-2 followed by PHQ-9 if positive
mcqs.explanation: Depression is common in HF (up to 40%) and worsens outcomes. The two-question PHQ-2 screen is recommended, followed by PHQ-9 if positive. Depression in HF is treatable and screening improves outcomes.
mcqs.q: A patient with heart failure and suspected depression should be screened with:
mcqs.correct: PHQ-2 followed by PHQ-9 if positive

options: Direct specialist consultations bypassing primary care
mcqs.explanation: Guided Care works within primary care — a nurse works with 2-5 physicians. It provides home assessment, care plans, monthly monitoring, self-care promotion, transitional care, caregiver support, and community resource access.
mcqs.q: Guided Care model for patients with multiple chronic conditions provides all EXCEPT:
mcqs.correct: Direct specialist consultations bypassing primary care

mcqs.explanation: Echocardiography is first-line to assess ventricular function, valvular disease, and wall motion. Neither CXR nor ECG alone can determine the specific cardiac abnormality causing HF.
mcqs.q: The most appropriate initial imaging for new heart failure diagnosis is:

mcqs.explanation: Radionuclide ventriculography accurately measures LV function and RV EF, but CANNOT directly assess valvular abnormalities or cardiac hypertrophy.
mcqs.q: Radionuclide ventriculography provides accurate measurement of:

options: It is a summary measure of overall health impact guiding care planning
mcqs.explanation: Functional status summarizes overall impact of health conditions. Changes should prompt diagnostic evaluation and intervention. Drives care planning including goals of care.
mcqs.q: In frail elderly with HF, functional status assessment is essential because:
mcqs.correct: It is a summary measure of overall health impact guiding care planning

mcqs.explanation: Value-based care features risk sharing, quality measurement, care coordination, and population health management. Fee-for-service incentivizes excess care and is what value-based care aims to replace.
mcqs.q: Value-based care in geriatrics features all EXCEPT:

options: Providers lose money for poor outcomes
Providers can benefit financially for high-value care but aren't liable for losses
Patients pay more out of pocket
mcqs.explanation: Upside (one-sided) risk: providers receive bonuses for meeting quality standards and delivering care under benchmark. No liability for losses. Less uncertainty but more limited gains.
mcqs.q: Upside risk in value-based care means:
mcqs.correct: Providers can benefit financially for high-value care but aren't liable for losses

options: Are beneficial and part of standard therapy
mcqs.explanation: ACE-I/ARBs are cornerstone of HF therapy. Beneficial across age groups. Start low, go slow in elderly. Monitor potassium and creatinine. Not automatically contraindicated with CKD.
mcqs.q: ACE inhibitors in elderly heart failure:
mcqs.correct: Are beneficial and part of standard therapy

mcqs.explanation: NSAIDs promote sodium and water retention, can worsen HF, counteract diuretics, and may reduce effectiveness of ACE-I. Listed in Beers Criteria for HF patients.
